question: What type of stream does the req (IncomingMessage) object represent in http.createServer?

options.text: Readable Stream -- request body data arrives in chunks via data/end events
Writable Stream -- the server response is written to it
options.explanation: Writable Stream is the res (ServerResponse) object. The req object is a Readable Stream from which the request body is read

options.text: Duplex Stream -- reads and writes data simultaneously
options.explanation: req is only a Readable Stream. Duplex Streams are used in WebSocket and TCP connections, not in HTTP requests

options.text: Transform Stream -- transforms data on the fly
options.explanation: Transform Streams are used for data transformation (e.g., compression). req is a Readable Stream for reading incoming data

question: Why should you use fs.createReadStream().pipe(res) instead of fs.readFileSync() for serving large files?

options.text: readFileSync loads the entire file into RAM, while pipe transfers data in chunks (~64 KB), saving memory
readFileSync doesn't support binary files
options.explanation: readFileSync works perfectly with binary files. The problem is that for a 500 MB file, readFileSync loads all 500 MB into RAM simultaneously

options.text: pipe is faster because it skips access permission checks
options.explanation: pipe doesn't skip any checks. Its advantage is streaming data in chunks with backpressure management, which is critical under high load

options.text: readFileSync blocks the event loop, but pipe also blocks -- there's no difference
options.explanation: readFileSync does block the event loop. But pipe works asynchronously without blocking the event loop, plus saves memory through chunked transfer

question: How do you properly protect against path traversal attacks (../../etc/passwd) when serving static files?

options.text: Use path.resolve() and verify the resulting path starts with publicDir
Check that the URL doesn't contain '..' characters
options.explanation: A simple '..' check is insufficient -- attackers can use URL encoding (%2e%2e), double encoding, or other bypasses. path.resolve() normalizes the path, and a startsWith check guarantees safety

options.text: Use encodeURIComponent for the URL
options.explanation: encodeURIComponent encodes URLs for sending but doesn't protect against path traversal. You need path.resolve() + checking the path is within the allowed directory

options.text: Reject requests longer than 100 characters
options.explanation: URL length limits don't protect against path traversal -- the attack ../../etc/passwd fits within 100 characters. You need path.resolve() + startsWith(publicDir)

question: What happens if you don't call res.end() in the http.createServer handler?

options.text: The connection will hang until the client disconnects by timeout -- the browser will wait indefinitely
Node.js will automatically end the response after 30 seconds
options.explanation: Node.js doesn't auto-end responses. Without res.end(), the connection stays open until the client closes it by timeout or the user cancels the request

options.text: The server will crash with ERR_RESPONSE_NOT_ENDED
options.explanation: No such error exists. Without res.end(), the connection simply remains open -- it's a resource leak, not a server crash

options.text: The client will receive an empty response with code 200
options.explanation: Without res.end(), the client receives nothing -- the connection stays open waiting for data. An empty response is only sent with an explicit res.end() call

question: Which Content-Type headers should be specified for different request body types?

options.text: application/json for JSON data
application/x-www-form-urlencoded for HTML forms
multipart/form-data for file uploads
text/html for all POST requests
options.explanation: text/html is a Content-Type for HTML pages in server responses. For POST requests, use application/json, application/x-www-form-urlencoded, or multipart/form-data depending on the data
